name: Charlie Root
hof: Not inducted. Cubs franchise all-time leader in wins (201), games pitched (605), and innings pitched (3,137). 5× MVP consideration. The most accomplished Cubs pitcher in history, overshadowed by one pitch.

real_stats.ws_record: 0-3, 6.75 ERA in 6 WS games (4 World Series: 1929, 1932, 1935, 1938 — all losses)

stat_justification.stf: Root was described as 'cool and graceful' with a 'mysterious delivery.' He threw overhand, three-quarters, and sidearm, keeping hitters off-balance. His fastball was elite for the era, and his curveball — which he called his 'wrinkle ball' ('It slews a little like the wrinkle in a piece of suit') — froze hitters. He ranked top-5 in the NL in K/9 for six consecutive years (1926-31). His 3.76 ERA in 1927 looks pedestrian, but he threw 309 IP and led the NL in wins. The Sporting News praised his 'mysterious delivery.' Rating of 3.
stat_justification.ctl: Root led the NL in walks (117) in 1927 — his wildness was real. BB/9 of 3.41 is tier 1 at best. He was an aggressive, intimidating pitcher who threw inside relentlessly (his nickname 'Chinski' came from throwing at batters' chins), and that approach came with walks. His control improved significantly later in his career — 2.60 ERA in 1933 with just 44 walks — but in his peak year, control was his clear weakness. Rating of 1.
stat_justification.sta: 309 IP in 1927 — led the NL. 48 games — led the NL. 26 complete games. McCarthy trusted Root more than any other pitcher; he started every 4th day and occasionally relieved between starts. Averaged 252 IP per season from 1926-33. Career 3,197 IP, 177 CG, 21 SHO. The ultimate workhorse who carried the Cubs staff for a decade. Rating of 3 (maximum).
stat_justification.def: Root was a competent fielder with a .965 fielding percentage in 1927, and he held his position well. But he had no particular defensive reputation and no awards. Not a factor. Rating of 0.
stat_justification.clu: CATASTROPHIC in October. 0-3 with a 6.75 ERA across 6 World Series games. In the 1929 WS Game 4, he held an 8-0 lead through 6 innings before the A's scored 10 runs to win 10-8 — one of the greatest collapses in WS history (Root was pulled after allowing 6 runs in the 7th). In the 1932 WS Game 3, he gave up the 'Called Shot' to Babe Ruth — whether Ruth pointed or not, Root gave up the HR that became the most famous in baseball history. In the 1935 WS Game 2, he was knocked out before recording an out in the first inning. He died angry about the Called Shot, insisting until his last breath that Ruth didn't point. Rating of 0 (minimum).

personality.leadership_style: The Staff Ace as Enforcer. Root led by intimidation, durability, and sheer competitiveness. He wasn't the pitcher who talked to you about your mechanics — he was the pitcher who took the ball every fourth day, threw 300 innings, and dared you to match his effort. Teammates called him 'Old Bear.' His manager McCarthy depended on him more than any other pitcher for five years. His pitching philosophy, later codified as the 'Nine Hill Commandments,' prioritized conditioning, ball control, fielding, and — above all — heart.
personality.temperament: FIERCE. Root was a cigar-chomping, tobacco-spitting, chin-throwing brawler on the mound. His nickname 'Chinski' came from his habit of throwing at hitters' chins — he threw inside with malicious intent and dared hitters to dig in. He was described as having a 'no-nonsense visage' that was 'one of the most intimidating tools in his baseball arsenal.' Off the mound, he was a quiet, blue-collar man who quit school at 13 to drive a grocery wagon. The fierceness was professional, not personal — but it was absolute.
personality.work_ethic: RELENTLESS. Root quit school at 13 and worked as a grocery boy, shipping clerk, and wood pattern maker before baseball. He went 0-4 with a 5.70 ERA in his first MLB stint (1923 Browns), was sent to the minors, won 46 games in two PCL seasons, and came back to win 201 in the majors. He pitched in organized baseball until age 49. After playing, he became a minor league manager and major league pitching coach, coaching the Braves to a World Series title in 1957. His career spanned 35 years in professional baseball.
personality.lifestyle: Blue-collar Midwest roots. Eighth of nine children from Middletown, Ohio. After baseball, he became a cattle rancher with a 1,000-acre Diamond-R Ranch in Paicines, California. Liked hunting and fishing. Married to baseball and the land. No flash, no fame-seeking — just a working man who happened to throw baseballs for a living.
personality.era_adaptability: MODERATE. Root's stuff was elite for his era — great fastball, wicked curve, multiple arm angles. But his control issues (117 BB in 1927) would be more punishing in the modern game. His innings-eating durability (309 IP) wouldn't be utilized today. He'd profile as a mid-rotation starter with a nasty approach — a Marcus Stroman or Zack Greinke type. His intimidation factor would still translate. His willingness to pitch inside would make him a modern analytics darling for 'establishing the zone.'
personality.clubhouse_impact: THE BACKBONE. Root was the Cubs for 16 years — longer than any other pitcher in franchise history. He was there for all four World Series (1929, 1932, 1935, 1938). He was the constant while managers changed (McCarthy, Hornsby, Grimm). Phil Cavarretta said Root 'was a big help, not only as a pitcher, but as a coach to our younger pitchers.' He was the elder statesman, the guy who'd been through everything, who'd taken the ball when it mattered most — even if the results in October weren't what he wanted.
personality.dark_side: THE CALLED SHOT. This is the wound that never healed. On October 1, 1932, in Game 3 of the World Series, Babe Ruth stepped to the plate against Root with the count 2-2. Ruth gestured — toward the pitcher? Toward center field? — and then hit the next pitch for a monstrous home run. The moment became the most famous in baseball history, and Root became the answer to the most humiliating trivia question: 'Who gave up the Called Shot?' Root denied it until his dying day, insisting Ruth pointed at him, not at the bleachers. Frank Crosetti (Ruth's own shortstop) backed Root up. But the myth won. Root's 201 wins, his Cubs records, his fierce competitiveness — all erased by one pitch. He reportedly said he would have knocked Ruth down if Ruth had really pointed. In ILB: Root carries the 'Called Shot' trait — a permanent -2 to legacy and a chance that any dramatic HR against him becomes legendary, overshadowing everything else he's done.

chemistry_traits.desc: Throws at chins. +1 intimidation vs all hitters. Hitters with CLU < 2 lose -1 CON against Root. But +15% chance of hit-by-pitch and potential ejection.
Permanent -2 legacy. Any HR against Root in a high-profile game has a 20% chance of becoming 'legendary' — permanently associated with Root's name for the wrong reasons.
Grizzled, durable, fierce. +1 STA and +1 intimidation. Root takes the ball every day and growls at anyone who suggests rest.
Takes the ball every 4th day without complaint. +1 to team's starting rotation cohesion. McCarthy trusts him above all others.
After retirement, Root becomes an elite pitching coach. +2 to any young pitcher's development. Led Braves to lowest NL ERA (1956) and WS title (1957).
0-3 with 6.75 ERA in the World Series. -2 to all stats in World Series play. Cannot be trusted in elimination games.
Root insists Ruth didn't point. He carries this anger to his grave. +1 motivation in regular season (proves his worth), -1 composure when the Called Shot is mentioned.
Root's curveball 'slews a little like the wrinkle in a piece of suit.' +1 STF against right-handed hitters who haven't faced the pitch before.

preferred_locations.note: 309 IP, 48 games, 26 CG in 1927 alone. He lives here.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'The Mound' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Practice Field/HIGH as the mound-craft domain. Seventh mound-craft Practice Field/HIGH in the set (Mathewson, McGinnity, Phillippe, Plank, Reulbach, Reynolds, Roe, Root). Root's is the highest-volume register: defined by pure innings accumulation (309 IP, 48 G, 26 CG in a single season) rather than deception (Roe), craft (Mathewson), or iron-man stamina (McGinnity). The 'he lives here' note is the most territorial framing in the mound-craft domain.

preferred_locations.note: 16 years as a Cub. Franchise career leader in W, G, IP.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Wrigley Field' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Media/Spotlight/HIGH as the franchise-identity-stage domain. Root's 16-year tenure as the Cubs' all-time leader in wins (201), games (605), and innings (3,137) is the most statistically definitive franchise-record holder in the set's Media/Spotlight/HIGH domain. The dark irony: the same franchise stage that defines his achievement also defines his humiliation (the Called Shot, also at Wrigley).

preferred_locations.note: 'Chinski' — the pitch near the chin. His territory.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Inside Corner' / affinity 'HIGH' — first pitch-location preferred_location in the set. 'The Inside Corner' is a game-mechanics space defined positively as a domain of craft-and-intimidation ownership. Prior game-mechanics-space preferred_locations: Reynolds's 'Home Plate (incoming)' / LOW (injury domain); Rice's 'The Outfield Gaps' / MAXIMUM (speed-geometry domain). Root's Inside Corner is the first pitch-location entry and the first game-mechanics-space HIGH defined as aggressive craft rather than injury or geometry. Mapped to Practice Field/HIGH as the intimidation-craft sub-domain. The chemistry_trait 'Chinski' (+1 intimidation, -1 CON to CLU<2 hitters) is the mechanical expression.

preferred_locations.note: 1,000-acre Diamond-R Ranch in Paicines, CA. Hunting, fishing, ranching.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Cattle Ranch' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Community Events/HIGH as the post-baseball rural-life domain. Fourth rural-origin Community Events/HIGH in the set (Manush, Reynolds, Roe, Root). Root's is the most acreage-specific: the 1,000-acre Diamond-R Ranch is the largest named landholding in any preferred_location in the set. The action card 'The 1,000-Acre Ranch' and personality.lifestyle ('cattle rancher with a 1,000-acre Diamond-R Ranch') are cross-field expressions. 'Hunting, fishing, ranching' — the most activity-dense rural HIGH note in the set.

preferred_locations.note: Pitched in organized ball until 49. Managed. Coached. Baseball lifer.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Minor Leagues / Coaching' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Community Events/HIGH as the sustained-baseball-career domain. Second coaching-domain preferred_location in the set (Robinson had Practice Field/HIGH for the mound-as-classroom; this is the broader baseball-life career arc as a domain). Root's 35-year professional baseball career (player to age 49, minor league manager, major league coach) is the longest career-span preferred_location in the set. The chemistry_trait 'Nine Hill Commandments' (+2 to young pitcher development, 1957 WS ring as coach) is the mechanical expression of this domain's value.

preferred_locations.note: 0-3, 6.75 ERA. The one place that destroyed him.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'World Series' / affinity 'LOW' — mapped to Media/Spotlight/LOW as the catastrophic-public-performance domain. Second temporal-event preferred_location in the set (Rice's '1938 World Series' was first). Root's 'World Series' LOW is broader: it encompasses four WS appearances (1929, 1932, 1935, 1938) rather than a single year. The chemistry_trait 'October Collapse' (-2 to all WS stats) and momentum.pressure_response ('INVERTED') are the cross-field expressions. Root and Rice are the only cards with a World Series as a LOW domain.

preferred_locations.note: Remembered for the Called Shot, not 201 wins. The cruelest legacy in baseball.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'History Books' / affinity 'LOW' — first abstract/meta-referential preferred_location in the set. 'History Books' is not a physical place, a temporal event, or a game-mechanics space — it is the player's own historical reputation as a domain. The most self-aware LOW label in the set: Root's awareness that his biography has been distorted by a single pitch is encoded as a preferred_location affinity. Mapped to Media/Spotlight/LOW as the legacy-distortion domain. Only the second player in the set with two Media/Spotlight/LOWs (Reulbach was first, with HOF-absence LOW and Shadows/Anonymity LOW). Root's double LOW encodes both catastrophic performance (World Series) and reputational distortion (History Books) as distinct domain-failures.

hot_triggers: Regular season dominance — 26 wins in 1927, averaged 18 wins/year for 8 seasons
Intimidation working — hitters backing off the plate, free swings becoming defensive
Heavy workload — Root got BETTER the more he pitched. 309 IP was his best year.
Team pennant races — Root won 4 straight starts during the Cubs' 1935 21-game win streak
cold_triggers: World Series pressure — 0-3 with 6.75 ERA. Complete October collapse.
The Called Shot narrative — any time the legend is invoked, Root's composure fractures
Overweight in spring — reported 15 lbs heavy in 1928, battled weight all year, went 14-18
Poor run support — in 1926, his team scored 2 or fewer in 13 of his losses (13 total runs)
momentum.pressure_response: INVERTED. Root in the regular season was one of the most dependable pitchers in baseball — 26 wins, 4th in MVP, staff ace for a decade. But in October, he completely collapsed. The 1929 WS Game 4 is the nightmare: Cubs led 8-0 through 6 innings, then the A's scored 10 runs to win (Root gave up 6 in the 7th before being pulled). The Called Shot in 1932. Knocked out in the 1st inning of the 1935 WS. In ILB: Root is the anti-clutch pitcher — elite in April through September, catastrophic in October. He's the ace who gets you to the World Series and then can't win it. Draft him as your #1 starter with the understanding that you need someone else for October.

action_card_seeds.title: The Called Shot
action_card_seeds.text: World Series, Game 3. Your ace faces the greatest hitter in baseball. Count 2-2. The hitter gestures — at you? At center field? He hits the next pitch into the bleachers. The moment becomes the most famous in baseball history. Your pitcher's 201 wins are forgotten. He becomes a trivia answer. He denies the gesture until he dies. The truth doesn't matter — the myth wins.
action_card_seeds.origin: October 1, 1932. Wrigley Field. Ruth vs Root. The Called Shot. Root denied it for 38 years until his death. Crosetti (Ruth's own shortstop) said Ruth pointed at Root, not center field. The debate continues forever.

action_card_seeds.title: The Ten-Run Inning
action_card_seeds.text: Your ace has an 8-0 lead through 6 innings of a World Series game. He's cruising. Then in the 7th, everything falls apart. The opponent scores 10 runs. Your ace gives up 6 before being pulled. The greatest collapse in World Series history. The lead is gone. The series is gone. The memory is permanent.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1929 World Series Game 4. Cubs led A's 8-0. Root was pulled in the 7th after giving up 6 runs. The A's completed the rally and won 10-8. Still considered one of the greatest comebacks in WS history.

action_card_seeds.title: The Grocery Boy
action_card_seeds.text: A 13-year-old kid quits school to drive a grocery wagon. He plays baseball in his spare time. He works as a shipping clerk, a wood pattern maker, a grocery delivery boy. Then he becomes a professional pitcher and wins 201 games. The kid who couldn't afford school becomes a franchise legend.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root quit school at 13 in Middletown, Ohio. He worked multiple blue-collar jobs before baseball gave him a career. He still listed 'wood pattern maker, shipping clerk, and grocery boy' as his major occupations in a 1938 questionnaire.

action_card_seeds.title: Chinski
action_card_seeds.text: Your pitcher throws one under the hitter's chin. The hitter goes down. Next pitch, the hitter is leaning back. Easy strike. Your pitcher does this all game — brush back, then the wrinkle ball curve on the outside corner. Hitters hate him. The ump warns him once. He spits tobacco juice and throws inside again.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root's nickname 'Chinski' (given by Charlie Grimm) came from his habit of throwing at hitters' chins. He combined inside fastballs with his 'wrinkle ball' curveball to devastate hitters. It was described as 'one of the most intimidating tools in his arsenal.'

action_card_seeds.title: The Nine Hill Commandments
action_card_seeds.text: Your retired ace becomes a pitching coach. He writes down nine commandments for his pitchers: conditioning, ball control, fielding, mastering your pitches before trying new ones, running every day, bunting, pacing, developing a changeup, and — finally — heart. His staff leads the league in ERA. His team wins the World Series. The ring he could never win as a player, he wins as a coach.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root's 'Nine Hill Commandments' were his coaching philosophy. As Braves pitching coach, his staff led the NL in ERA in 1956 and won the 1957 World Series — giving Root his only championship ring, as a coach rather than a player.

action_card_seeds.title: The 1,000-Acre Ranch
action_card_seeds.text: After 35 years in professional baseball — player, manager, coach — your man retires to a 1,000-acre cattle ranch. He hunts. He fishes. He raises cattle. He never talks about the Called Shot unless asked, and when asked, he denies it. He carries the anger quietly, like a man carrying fence posts.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root retired to his Diamond-R Ranch in Paicines, California — 1,000 acres of cattle country. He died there in 1970, still insisting Ruth never pointed.

action_card_seeds.title: The Last Man Born in the 1800s
action_card_seeds.text: Your pitcher wins a game, and it's noted that he's the last man born in the 19th century to win a Major League game. An era ends with a final victory.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root, born March 17, 1899, was the last player born in the 1800s to record a MLB win.

action_card_seeds.title: 201 Wins, Zero Fame
action_card_seeds.text: Your franchise's all-time wins leader is not in the Hall of Fame. He led the league in wins. He went to four World Series. He pitched for 17 years. But he's remembered for one pitch that someone else hit. The most wins in franchise history, and the most painful legacy in the game.
action_card_seeds.origin: Root holds Cubs career records for wins (201), games (605), and innings (3,137). He was never seriously considered for the Hall of Fame. His legacy is the Called Shot.

art_direction.face: Weathered, compact, fierce. 5'10" 190 lbs — stocky, powerful, built low to the ground like a bull. Not a tall, graceful pitcher — a pit fighter with a baseball. Age 28 in 1927. Hard jaw, tobacco-stained sneer. Cigar-chomping intensity. The face of a man who quit school at 13, drove a grocery wagon, and then threw at your chin for a living. Not cruel — determined. The ferocity is professional, earned, blue-collar.
art_direction.attire: Chicago Cubs 1927 road grays or home whites. Mid-delivery on the mound — that violent, three-quarters arm slot, ball about to leave his hand. Or standing on the mound, glaring at a hitter who just backed off the plate. Tobacco juice on the uniform. The mound as his domain, his hill, his kingdom.
art_direction.mood: Defiant. Root's card should radiate a challenge: 'Hit this if you can.' There's anger in it — not wild anger, but the cold, focused anger of a man who will throw 309 innings and dare the world to knock him off the mound. The mood should also carry a hint of tragedy — the man who did everything right for 17 years and is remembered for one pitch.
art_direction.style: Warm sepia with darker, grittier tones than the other Bashers cards. Root's card should feel rougher, more worn — like a newspaper clipping found in a barn. Less golden, more tobacco-stained. The contrast with Hartnett (the Cubs teammate) should be stark: Hartnett is the beloved institution, Root is the forgotten workhorse.
art_direction.reference: Think of a Depression-era photograph — a man who works with his hands and his back. Root is the most blue-collar player on the Bashers roster. The card should feel like it's been through something. Wrigley Field in the background, but darker, more autumnal — the October where everything went wrong.
